`why` is a code-archaeology CLI that answers "why does this code exist?" by analyzing git history and synthesizing explanations via LLM. It provides risk assessment, ownership signals, and change context before you modify unfamiliar code.
- **Prevents accidental deletions:** Understand why code was written before removing "dead-looking" functions
- **Risk-aware changes:** Get HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW risk signals based on commit history and keywords
- **Ownership discovery:** Find who knows the code and bus-factor risks with `--team`
- **Co-change awareness:** See coupled files before broad refactors with `--coupled`
- **Incident context:** Link code to past hotfixes, security patches, and incidents
- **LLM-powered synthesis:** One LLM call per query with structured git data as input
# Validate config and test LLM connectivity
| `why <target>` | Basic archaeology query with LLM synthesis |
| `why <target> --blame-chain` | Walk past mechanical edits to true origin |
| `why <target> --rename-safe` | Assess whether Rust symbol rename is safe |
| `why time-bombs` | Aged TODOs and expired markers |
| `why onboard --limit 10` | Top symbols for new engineers |
| `why pr-template` | Generate PR template from staged diff |
| `why config init` | Interactive setup (main entry point) |
| `why config get` | Show current effective config |
| `why doctor` | Validate config and test LLM |
Check risk level and history before touching.
See coupled files and ownership before planning scope.
Check caller risk to assess rename safety.
Review staged changes and generate template.
| **HIGH** | Stop and investigate. Likely security, incident, or critical business logic. |
| **MEDIUM** | Review carefully. Migration, integration, or compatibility-sensitive code. |
| **LOW** | Routine code. Standard review practices apply. |
Symbol resolution works for: Rust (`.rs`), Go (`.go`), JavaScript (`.js`), TypeScript (`.ts`, `.tsx`), Java (`.java`), Python (`.py`)
- Cache keys include `HEAD` hash prefix for natural invalidation
- **"could not find repository"**: Run from inside a git repo, or use repo-wide commands like `why doctor` from anywhere
- **Ambiguous symbol**: Use qualified name like `Module::function` instead of just `function`
- **No LLM synthesis**: Check `why doctor` for config/auth issues; fallback to `--no-llm` for heuristic-only
- **Missing credentials**: Run `why config init` or set `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`/`OPENAI_API_KEY`/`ZAI_API_KEY`
- **Always run `why` before deleting unfamiliar code** — it may be a security fix or incident response
- Treat `HIGH` risk output as a stop-and-investigate signal
- Use `--blame-chain` to find true origin, not just last mechanical edit
- Use `--no-llm` in CI or when LLM is unavailable
- Use `why doctor` to diagnose config/auth issues

`linehash` is a file editing tool that uses content-hashed line anchors (`12:ab3f`) instead of fragile exact-text matching. It's designed for agent-driven editing where concurrent changes are expected and edit safety is critical.
- **Stable anchors:** Uses `line:hash` format that survives nearby edits—line numbers shift but hashes stay valid
- **Concurrent-safe:** Detects stale anchors when content changed; fails explicitly instead of guessing
- **Audit trail:** Optional `--receipt` and `--audit-log` for tracking edit history
- **No merge conflicts:** Each edit is independent; no patch files that conflict
- **Works with any text:** Language-agnostic; no parsing required
- **content_hash**: First 4+ chars of SHA-256 of line content (for stability)
| `linehash read <file>` | Show file with line:hash anchors |
| `linehash read <file> --anchor 42:a3f2` | Show context around specific anchor |
| `linehash index <file>` | Show just anchors, no content |
| `linehash annotate <file> <query>` | Find and annotate matching lines |
| `linehash move <file> <anchor> <target-anchor>` | Move line to new position |
| `linehash from-diff <diff-file>` | Convert diff to anchor edits |

Open terminal. Run `claude`. Paste the init prompt — the one that loads context, explains the project, tells the agent what it's working on. Wait for it to understand. Finally, the agent starts working.
You exit. You open a new session. You paste the init prompt again. Load context again. Wait again. Resume where it left off — manually, because nothing remembers.
You can't leave. You can't do other work. You sit there, watching, waiting for the next context limit so you can loop it again. The agent does the work. But you're the one who can't walk away.
...or until you give up for the night
You become the orchestrator. A human one. Manually chaining sessions, one at a time, unable to stop because the moment you step away the work stops too.

Define your tasks with `br`. Type `grove run`. Walk away. Come back to completed work — sessions handled, context rotations managed, and native handoffs, transcript archive, and playbook memory carried forward automatically. Grove didn't appear from nothing. The exit gate and circuit breaker come from [Frank Bria](https://github.com/frankbria)'s [ralph-claude-code](https://github.com/frankbria/ralph-claude-code), which proved that an autonomous Claude loop needs both heuristic detection and an explicit exit signal to avoid premature stops. The entire task graph — dependencies, lifecycle, ready-queue — runs on [Jeff Emanuel](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone)'s [beads_rust](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust), and grove uses his [beads_viewer](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_viewer) for PageRank, critical path, and triage scoring when deciding which bead to dispatch next. Grove's native transcript archive is modeled after his [coding_agent_session_search](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/coding_agent_session_search), and the playbook engine — evidence scoring, confidence decay, curation, anti-pattern inversion — is adapted directly from his [cass_memory_system](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/cass_memory_system). His [ntm](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/ntm) shaped early thinking about session layout and parallel coordination, though grove chose direct process spawning over tmux. Finally, [nwiizo](https://github.com/nwiizo)'s [ccswarm](https://github.com/nwiizo/ccswarm) influenced the Rust workspace structure, type-state patterns, and task scoring design. Thank you all.
*End-to-end Grove workflow from bead creation to mirrored completion.*

Grove runs a continuous autonomous loop over your beads task graph. Each bead is dispatched to the configured provider runtime session. The coordinator can keep multiple sessions in flight concurrently up to `max_parallel`, while still enforcing file reservation safety and a single active leader lease. When context exhausts, grove checkpoints and spawns a fresh session automatically. Child beads inherit structured handoffs from parents.
├─ score candidates (priority + critical path + bv triage insights)
│     session A: claude -p "<task + parent handoffs + archive snippets + playbook rules>"
│     session B: claude -p "<task + parent handoffs + archive snippets + playbook rules>"
├─ session A outputs GROVE_EXIT: true (+ completion indicators met)
│     → index transcript into grove's native archive
│     → mirror to br (`br comment add` + `br close`)
│     → child bead C (depends on A) becomes ready
│     → spawns fresh session B' with checkpoint + full context injected
└─ loop until all beads done or shutdown
Grove does not exit just because Claude says it's done. It uses a **dual-condition check**:
1. `completion_indicators >= 2` — heuristic from natural language patterns in output
2. Claude's explicit `GROVE_EXIT: true` in the protocol block
Loop 5: "Phase complete, moving to next feature"
→ GROVE_EXIT: false (Claude says more work needed)
→ Result: SUCCESS → persist handoff, unblock children
This prevents premature exits during productive iterations.
Grove monitors each session for stuck loops:
No progress for 3 iterations → circuit OPEN
Same error repeated 5 times  → circuit OPEN
Permission denied 2 times    → circuit OPEN, fail fast
Auto-recovery: OPEN → cooldown (30min) → HALF_OPEN → test one iteration → CLOSED.
When context fills up, grove spawns a **brand new session** with full memory reconstructed:
→ grove persists checkpoint to DB + file
→ new session spawned with checkpoint + parent handoffs + archive snippets + playbook rules
→ work resumes mid-task in fresh context window
→ grove synthesizes emergency checkpoint from latest protocol state
Grove owns its memory entirely. No external memory or search tool is required.
→ grove indexes the transcript into its native FTS5 archive with transcript-backed provenance
→ grove persists a structured handoff (summary, artifacts, lessons, decisions, warnings)
→ grove extracts `GROVE_LESSONS` into playbook draft bullets
→ archive search: "auth middleware" → returns relevant snippets from past sessions
→ playbook selector: returns proven rules matching this task's scope/tags
→ Bead B starts knowing exactly what A did

Over time, repeated lessons get promoted (Candidate → Established → Proven). Harmful rules get demoted or inverted into anti-patterns. The playbook stays compact and self-curating via exponential decay scoring.
Grove also records reaction evaluations on failure paths and can persist recovery capsules plus retry-oriented guidance, so the recovery loop is no longer purely static policy metadata.
- Provider CLI: `claude` or `codex` (install the one you plan to run Grove with)
No other orchestration, memory, or search tool is required. Grove implements all memory and retrieval natively.

`grove init` creates a user-owned startup prompt template at `.grove/startup_prompt.md` if it does not already exist. Edit that file to change the baseline instructions Grove injects into every freshly spawned session. Re-running `grove init` will preserve your edited file unless you delete it yourself. If Grove is already initialized, use `grove sync` to refresh the bead cache instead of re-running `grove init`.
Use `grove migrate --provider codex` or `grove migrate --provider claude` to switch an existing workspace between providers without resetting unrelated Grove settings.

If you pass `grove init --skills`, Grove scaffolds all bundled skills into `.agents/skills/<skill-name>/SKILL.md`. Each scaffold is create-if-missing and becomes user-owned immediately, so reruns (including `--force`) preserve any edits you make there.

{"id":"grove-2qc","title":"Implement event-driven reactions system for autonomous recovery","description":"Create a configurable, event-driven reaction system that automatically responds to triggers (circuit open, no progress, mirror failed) with autonomous actions - NO human notification.

Why this matters:
- Grove needs configurable automation for failure modes instead of hardcoded recovery logic. Reactions make recovery strategies declarative and inspectable.

## Design
Planned scope:
- Define ReactionTrigger enum: CircuitOpen, NoProgress, AgentIdle, ContextPressureHigh, MirrorFailed.
- Define ReactionAction enum: InjectRescue, RetryWithMutation, ForceCheckpoint, EnqueueMirrorRetry, ScheduleBackoff (NO NotifyUser).
- Implement ReactionRule struct with trigger, action, auto=true, max_attempts, escalate_to.
- Load reactions from grove.toml config with sensible defaults.
- Wire reactions into the orchestrator tick loop for automatic response.

## Acceptance Criteria
Done when:
- Common failure triggers automatically invoke configured reactions.
- Reactions are configurable via grove.toml without code changes.
- NO reaction ever notifies a human - all actions are autonomous.
- Reaction history is logged for post-mortem analysis.

## Notes
Autonomous-first design:
- Reactions replace hardcoded retry logic with declarative rules.
- Escalation means 'try different strategy' not 'notify human'.
- Default reactions should cover: circuit_open → inject_rescue, no_progress_3x → retry_with_mutation, mirror_failed → enqueue_retry.

Future-self context:
- This is the heart of Grove's autonomous recovery philosophy.

{"id":"grove-ieu","title":"Implement escalation tiers for progressive autonomous strategy","description":"Create a tiered escalation system where each failure triggers a progressively different autonomous strategy - NOT escalation to human.

Why this matters:
- When autonomous recovery fails, Grove should try different strategies (rescue prompt → mutation → switch model) rather than repeating the same action or giving up immediately.

## Design
Planned scope:
- Define EscalationTier enum: FirstAttempt, SecondAttempt, ThirdAttempt, FinalAttempt, GiveUp.
- Define MutationStrategy enum: NarrowClaimedPaths, DifferentArchiveSnippet, AlternativeBeadContract, ReduceContextWindow, SwitchModel.
- Implement EscalationPolicy with max_tiers and tier_actions mapping.
- Track current tier per run in TaskRunRecord.
- On GiveUp tier, create RecoveryCapsule with full context.

## Acceptance Criteria
Done when:
- Each retry attempt uses a different strategy based on escalation tier.
- Tier progression is logged and inspectable.
- GiveUp tier creates a recovery capsule for future learning.
- NO tier ever escalates to human notification.

## Notes
Autonomous-first design:
- Escalation = 'try harder/smarter' not 'ask for help'.
- Default escalation ladder: 1st=continue, 2nd=inject_rescue, 3rd=narrow_paths, 4th=switch_model, give_up=capsule.
- Tier is reset on successful progress.

Future-self context:
- This builds on rescue cards (grove-1j9.6.7) and recovery capsules (grove-1j9.7.7).
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-20T05:17:19.375647809Z","created_by":"quangdang","updated_at":"2026-03-21T18:39:51.329657425Z","closed_at":"2026-03-21T18:39:51.329607988Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["area:orchestrator","area:recovery","phase:3"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"grove-ieu","depends_on_id":"grove-1j9.7.7","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-20T05:17:19.375647809Z","created_by":"quangdang","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"grove-ieu","depends_on_id":"grove-2qc","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-20T05:17:19.375647809Z","created_by":"quangdang","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":138,"issue_id":"grove-ieu","author":"quangdang46","text":"Strict verification result: this remains open.
